title: Tabs
subtitle: `:tabnew`, {key:gt}, {key:gT} — tabs are layouts of windows, not files.
summary: :tabnew creates a new tab. gt cycles forward; gT backward. Each tab can have its own arrangement of splits. Most users don't need tabs at all.

blocks.text: Vim's *tabs* are not what tabs mean in other editors. In Vim, a tab is a **layout of windows** — a saved arrangement of splits. The same buffer can appear in multiple tabs; closing a tab doesn't close the buffers it showed.

blocks.title: Tabs Are Workspaces, Not File Tabs
blocks.text:
If you've used VS Code or Sublime, *that* concept of a tab — one tab per file — maps to **buffers** in Vim, not tabs.

A Vim tab is a *workspace*: an independent set of splits. Use them when you want, say, "frontend code over here, backend over there, tests in a third workspace." Each tab can have its own splits showing different (or the same) buffers.

Most Vim users barely use tabs. Once you internalize buffers + splits, tabs feel redundant. They're available if you want them, but if every-file-is-a-tab is the model you're after, configure a buffer-line plugin instead.
{key::}{key:t}{key:a}{key:b}{key:e} {file} opens {file} in a new tab. {key::}{key:t}{key:a}{key:b}{key: }{key:s}{key:p} repeats the *current* window in a new tab — including its buffer.
